coredata.dc:title: Hemolysis of 40% hematocrit, Albunex®-supplemented human erythrocytes by pulsed ultrasound: Frequency, acoustic pressure and pulse length dependence
coredata.dc:creator: Brayman, Andrew A.
Strickler, Peggy L.
Luan, Huong
Barned, Susan L.
Raeman, Carol H.
Cox, Christopher
Miller, Morton W.
coredata.dc:description: The dependence of hemolysis produced by pulsed ultrasound on ultrasound frequency, acoustic pressure and pulse length was explored. Human erythrocytes (40% hematocrit; in Albunex®-supplemented autologous plasma) were exposed (60 s) to 20 or 200 μs pulses of ultrasound at frequencies of 1.02, 2.24 or 3.46 MHz and at peak negative pressures [P−] ranging from 0.0 to ∼3.0 MPa in 0.5 MPa increments. The duty factor was 0.01. At each frequency, hemolysis increased with increasing acoustic pressure and depended weakly on pulse duration. At relatively high acoustic pressures, hemolysis depended strongly on ultrasound frequency; at lower pressures, the frequency dependence was weaker. The potential clinical significance of ultrasonic hemolysis is discussed.

Acoustic cavitation

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ELSEVIER Ultrasound in Med. & Biol., Vol. 23, No. 8, pp. 1237-1250, 1997 Copyright 1997 World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology Printed in the USA. All rights reserved 0301-5629/97 $17.00 + .00 PII S0301-5629(97)00126-9 HEMOLYSIS OF 40% HEMATOCRIT, ALBUNEXSUPPLEMENTED HUMAN ERYTHROCYTES BY PULSED ULTRASOUND: FREQUENCY, ACOUSTIC PRESSURE AND PULSE LENGTH DEPENDENCE ANDREW A. BRAYMAN,? PEGGY L. STRICKLER,* HUONG LUAN,* SUSAN L. BARNED,t CAROL H. RAEMAN,* CHRISTOPHER COX* and MORTON W. MILLER* *Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, *Division of Biostatistics, and the *Rochester Center for Biomedical Ultrasound, The University of Rochester, Rochester, NY 14642 (Received 28 May 1997; in final form 17 June 1997) Abstract--The dependence of hemolysis produced by pulsed ultrasound on ultrasound frequency, acoustic pressure and pulse length was explored. Human erythrocytes (40% hematocrit; in Albunexsupplemented autologous plasma) were exposed (60 s) to 20 or 200 ~s pulses of ultrasound at frequencies of 1.02, 2.24 or 3.46 MHz and at peak negative pressures [P-] ranging from 0.0 to -3.0 MPa in 0.5 MPa increments. The duty factor was 0.01. At each frequency, hemolysis increased with increasing acoustic pressure and depended weakly on pulse duration. At relatively high acoustic pressures, hemolysis depended strongly on ultrasound frequency; at lower pressures, the frequency dependence was weaker. The potential clinical significance of ultrasonic hemolysis is discussed. 1997 World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology. Key Words: Pulsed ultrasound, Hemolysis, Albunex~, Ultrasound bioeffects, Acoustic cavitation. INTRODUCTION The development and implementation of the "Standard for Real-Time Display of Thermal and Mechanical Out- put Indices on Diagnostic Ultrasound Equipment" (AIUM/NEMA 1992) was founded in a perceived un- derstanding of the principal mechanisms of action asso- ciated with ultrasound-induced bioeffects. Two indices were proposed, one dealing with tissue heating (the ther- mal index; TI), and the other with mechanical effects related to inertial cavitation (the mechanical index; MI). They represent a change in general philosophy and reg- ulation with respect to the output of diagnostic equip- ment (vide Food and Drug Administration 1994). The older regulations [the 510(k) Guide; Food and Drug Administration 1985] specified that output levels could not exceed those used in 1976. The new regulations regarding the allowable extent of tissue heating are similar to the older ones; i.e., the permissible temporal average intensities remain much the same. However, for some applications, the new reg- ulations allow for the use of greater peak positive [P+] and peak negative [P-] acoustic pressures. Cavitation- related bioeffects are much more strongly related to peak exposure measures than to temporal average ones (Child et al. 1990; Carstensen et al. 1992; Raeman et al. 1993). Address for correspondence: Andrew A. Brayman, Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, School of Medicine and Dentistry, The University of Rochester, Rochester, NY 14642 USA. E-mail: aabrayman @ obgyn.rochester.edu The output limit defined by the new regulations can now be specified in terms of the MI, with an upper limit value of 1.9 (see Meltzer 1996). However, a recent survey of modern diagnostic ultrasound equipment indicates that some machines are capable of substantially exceeding this limit (Patton et al. 1994). The MI is unitless and is attributable to Holland and Apfel (1989, 1990) and Apfel and Holland (1991). The MI is based on theoretical treatments, performed using the conservative assumption that "optimal-sized" bub- bles are always present in the host fluid, which indicate that the threshold for acoustic cavitation is proportional to P- and inversely proportional to the center frequency, f, of the applied ultrasound. The MI is currently defined as p-/fo.5. Additionally, there is evidence that the extent of cavitation activity also depends on acoustic pressure and frequency (see, e.g., Flynn 1982; Flynn and Church 1988; Miller and Thomas 1993). Pulse duration may also be an important determinant of the extent of bioeffects produced by inertial cavitation or cavitation-like bubble activity (see, e.g., Flynn and Church 1988; Child et al. 1990; Carstensen et al. 1992; Brayman et al. 1996). The general objectives of this study were to: 1. Explore the frequency dependence of hemolysis [used as an indicator of the occurrence and extent of cavitation activity] over the range of 1-3.5 MHz (i.e., the lower end of the frequency range used diagnostically); 2. explore the dependence of hemolysis on P- within this fre- quency range; and 3. explore the dependence of hemo- lysis on pulse duration at constant duty factor. All ex- 1237 1238 Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology Volume 23, Number 8, 1997 perimental samples contained the microbubble contrast agent Albunex(Molecular Biosystems Inc., San Diego, CA, USA); such agents can nucleate cavitation activity and promote cavitation-related hemolysis in vitro (MW Miller et al. 1995; Miller and Thomas 1995, 1997; Bray- man et al. 1995, 1996) and in vivo (Dalecki et al. 1997). The specific hypotheses tested here were that hemolysis of 40% HCT human erythrocytes suspended in autolo- gous plasma supplemented with 3.6% (V:V) Albunex microspheres would decrease with increasing ultrasound frequency at constant P- and increase with increasing P- and pulse duration at constant frequency. These hy- potheses were supported. MATERIALS AND METHODS Blood cells Cell suspensions were prepared as described else- where (Brayman et al. 1996). Briefly, whole blood was drawn daily from a volunteer and anticoagulated with CPDA-1 (8.4 mL/60 mL whole blood). The blood was centrifuged (10,000 g x 10 min), the plasma and the buffy coat removed separately from the red cell pellet, and the red cells resuspended in autologous plasma to an hematocrit of --40%. The cell suspension was oxygen- ated with 100% 02 and maintained on ice. Five donors were used. There were 5 replicate experiments conducted at each of the ultrasound frequencies used; each donor contributed once to each group of replicate experiments. The surface tension of a bubble and host fluid viscosity influence the acoustic pressure thresholds for inertial cavitation such that reduced viscosity and re- duced surface tension decrease the pressure threshold (Holland and Apfel 1990; Allen et al. 1997). Therefore, the effect of the CPDA anticoagulant on the cell suspen- sion viscosity and the equilibrium (static) and minimum (dynamic) surface tension of plasma were explored. Blood was drawn into commercial evacuated blood- collection tubes containing powdered sodium heparin and mixed thoroughly with the anticoagulant. After pool- ing the tube contents, the heparinized blood was divided into 2 aliquots, one of which received CPDA-1 at the same rate (14 mL/100 mL whole blood) used in exper- imental preparations. These were then centrifuged at 10,000 g X 10 min. An aliquot of the plasma supernatant was taken from each (i.e., plasma+heparin [PH], plasma+heparin+CPDA [PHC]). These were used in surface tension measurements. The remaining plasma was used to formulate 40% HCT suspensions as de- scribed earlier (i.e., PH + erythrocytes from heparin- only aliquot, PHC + erythrocytes from heparin+CPDA aliquot). The cell suspensions were maintained briefly under refrigeration until used in viscosity determinations. Measurements of surface tension at 37 and fully- saturated humidity were done in PH and PHC with a Pulsating Bubble Surfactometer (Electronetics Corp., Amherst, NY) based on the original design of Enhorning (1977). In this apparatus, a small air bubble of known radius is formed in a sample of interest, and is either held constant in size or pulsated between known maximum and minimum radii of 0.55 and 0.40 mm (50% area compression for a sphere). During static or dynamic bubble studies, surface tension is calculated at a given position from the measured pressure drop across the air-water interface by Laplace's Law [Ap = 2(surface tension)/radius; Enhorning 1977]. In our studies, equi- librium surface tension was approximated as the value reached 6 min after initial formation of a static bubble in PH or PHC, after which the bubble was pulsated at a rate of 20 cycles/min and the surface tension at minimum bubble radius (minimum surface tension) was deter- mined as a function of time. Equilibrium surface tension was similar in PH (48 _+ 0.8 mN/m; mean _+ standard deviation) and PHC (44 1.3 mN/m). Plots of minimum surface tension vs. time (not shown) were almost iden- tical for PH and PHC, with minimum values of 22.6 1.6 mN/m (PH) and 22.5 --- 7.3 mN/m (PHC) reached after 20 min of pulsation. The surface tension behavior of PH and PHC appears to be dominated by the surface- active constituents of plasma, which include both pro- teins (typically -6000-8000 mg/dL) and lipids (-150- 250 mg phospholipids/dL plus additional neutral lipids including cholesterol; Lehninger 1975). A variety of proteins and lipids adsorb to equilibrium surface tensions in the range of those measured here for PH and PHC and, on dynamic compression, many lipid and protein films generate substantially lower surface tensions than reached during adsorption (Adamson 1982; Shah 1970). It is likely that both the plasma proteins and lipids contribute to the very similar surface tension character- istics observed for PH and PHC. The viscosity of the 40% HCT erythrocyte suspen- sions formulated in PH or PHC was determined by standard methods (i.e., measurement of the time required for a fixed volume of fluid to pass through a small aperture, with distilled water used as the calibration standard). The temperature of the apparatus and all test fluids was maintained at 21 _ 0.5; at 21, the vis- cosity of water is -0.978 cp. Measurements taken using water were reproducible to within 2%. Whereas CPDA had little effect on surface tension, it had a substantial effect on the viscosity of the cell suspensions at 21. Cell suspensions formulated in PH had a mean (SEM) viscosity of 4.81 __+ 0.13 cp (c.f, value of 5 cp for whole blood; Duck 1990), and that of suspensions formulated in PHC was 3.88 - 0.13 cp, or --20% lower than in the absence of CPDA. Briefly, the sample is contained in a cylindrical "window" of dialysis membrane (vol. --500/~L; diam. = 6.4 mm, h = 15 mm) bounded by a stainless-steel plug at the bottom and a stainless-steel tube at the top, through which sample loading/unloading occurs. Attenuation measurements conducted with a water-filled exposure vessel indicated that a single thickness of the dialysis membrane reduced P+ and P- by --2% relative to that measured at the same location with the vessel absent. A variable speed motor was used to rotate the exposure vessels at 200 rpm during sample insonation. Ultrasound exposures Ultrasound was produced by focused piezoelectric transducers operating in a degassed water bath and driven at their resonance frequencies (1.02, 2.24 and 3.46 MHz). Transducer characteristics and the acoustic pres- sures used experimentally are summarized in Table 1. Exposure conditions were matched across ultrasound frequencies on the basis of the P-, which ranged from 0.0 to 3.0 MPa. Dosimetry was conducted using an Imotec type 80-0.5-4.0 PVDF needle hydrophone (Imotec Me/3tech- nik, Warendorf, Germany) to measure P+ and P- at the focal plane, and was performed for each experimental run. The needle hydrophone was calibrated against a Marconi bilaminar PVDF hydrophone (Model Y337611, 1 mm active region; Marconi Research Centre, Chelms- ford, UK) at 1.02, 2.24 and 3.46 MHz and over a wide range of acoustic pressures. The calibration results are summarized in Fig. 1. At each frequency, the needle hydrophone tended to underestimate the acoustic pres- sure relative to the Marconi instrument, particularly at high acoustic pressures and atf > 1 MHz. At 1.02 MHz, the relationship was linear, PM = --0.022001 + (1.0254 P~), [r2 = 0.994]; at 2.24 and 3.46 MHz, the relationship was best-described by third order polyno- mial equations: PM = 0.036881 + (1.2341 PI) + (0.048918 X p2) + (0.0057768 X p3), [r2 = 0.996], at 2.24 MHz and PM = --0.080755 + (1.2401 X PI) -F (0.080794 X PI2) + (0.015179 Pl3), [r2 = 0.996], at 3.46 MHz, where PM and P! represent pressure indica- tions of the Marconi and Imotec instruments, respec- tively. The calibration curves so generated were suffi- ciently well-defined to permit conversion of subsequent data obtained with the needle hydrophone to "Marconi hydrophone-equivalent" pressures. Symbols represent experimental ob- servations; curves are those defined by the curve-fit equations given in the text. 42-dB range of input attenuation settings, converting these to "Marconi hydrophone-equivalent" pressures, and establishing the attenuation settings appropriate to yield the desired "Marconi hydrophone-equivalent" peak negative pressures. Samples were exposed to ultra- sound pulses having nominal P- of 0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5 or 3.0 MPa. All sample treatments were 60 s in duration, and conducted at room temperature. The pulse durations were 20 or 200/xs, and the duty factor was held constant at 0.01 by varying the PRF (500 or 50 Hz, respectively). Contrast agent additions All samples contained Albunexcontrast agent at a ratio of 36 /xL/mL suspension (see MW Miller et al. 1995). A previous study (Brayman et al. 1995) demon- strated that, in the absence of Albunex pulsed 1-MHz ultrasound having acoustic pressures of +4.7 and -2.7 MPa produced detectable hemolysis only at very low (--1%) hematocrit. The Albunexmicrosphere concen- tration as supplied is --4.2 108/mL (Bleeker et at. 1990); neglecting losses in sample preparation, there were thus -1.5 107 microspheres/mL in the final mixture. To prepare a sample, 500 /xL of erythrocyte suspension were transferred to a small vial containing 18 /.zL of Albunex Following gentle agitation, the mixture was transferred to the exposure vessel using a Pasteur pipette. Protocol Samples were positioned at the focal plane of the ultrasound apparatus, rotation was initiated and samples were insonated as described above. The vessel contents were then transferred to a microcentrifuge tube and al- lowed to stand for 3 h to allow more complete expression of hemolysis (see Azadniv et at. 1996). After centrifu- gation, an aliquot of the supernatant was assayed for hemoglobin (Hgbn) content by the method of Drabkin and Austin (1932). Osmotically-lysed aliquots of the stock suspension served as standards; the blank was produced using plasma reserved during initial prepara- tory steps. Levels of hemolysis in experimental samples were corrected for the mean background level of hemo- lysis (i.e., that due to handling, etc.) observed in sham- exposed samples from the same stock cell suspension as described elsewhere (Brayman et al. 1996); these sham- exposed samples were distinct from samples exposed to 0.0 MPa ultrasound (i.e., the latter represented "double shams", and were included in the protocol as a check on the background correction). Statistical procedures The data obtained were from 5 different donors. Blood samples from each donor were tested under all combinations of 3 different experimental conditions: 1. ultrasound frequency (3 levels); 2. peak negative acous- tic pressure (7 levels); and 3. pulse duration (2 levels). The basic goals of the analysis were comparison of the mean rates of hemolysis for the different levels of the 3 factors, alone and in combination, and to determine the threshold for hemolysis with respect to peak negative acoustic pressure. The standard way to assess the effects of a number of different experimental conditions is to use the analysis of variance (ANOVA). In the present case, the appropri- ate analysis was a 3-way repeated measures ANOVA, with donor considered to be a random effect (i.e., treating the 5 donors as a sample from a larger population of possible donors). The 3 experimental conditions were treated as fixed within-donor effects. The ANOVA in- cluded tests for each factor separately (tests for main effects), as well as tests for interactions among combi- nations of the 3 factors. To make the analysis more robust to violations of assumptions, the Huynh-Feldt correction to degrees of freedom was used wherever appropriate. In the data set, 2 observations at the lowest level of peak negative pressure (i.e., the "double sham" treat- ment) were missing for 2 subjects. The ANOVA in this (unbalanced) case is much more complicated. To have complete (balanced) data, these missing values were filled in using the mean of the corresponding peak neg- ative pressure values at the other ultrasound frequencies Hemolysis of Erythrocytes by Ultrasound A. The ANOVA indicated that the variability across the various conditions was not constant, and a logarithmic transfor- mation was employed to stabilize the variance. Because some of the hemolysis observations were slightly nega- tive due to the use of a correction for background rates of hemolysis, any value less than 0.01 was set to 0.005 before taking the logarithms. RESULTS Hemolysis increased generally as P- increased (Figs. 2-4). At 1.02 MHz (Fig. 2), hemolysis remained at relatively low levels over the P- range of 0.0-2.0 MPa, and increased rapidly with further increase in P-. How- ever, even at P < 2.0 MPa, detectable hemolysis oc- curred (Fig. 2 inset). At 2.24 MHz, hemolysis did not increase sharply at any P used here (Fig. 3), but in- creased as an approximately linear function of P over the range of 0.0-3.0 MPa. The maximum level of he- molysis observed at 2.24 MHz (-2.3% at 3.0 MPa, 200 /xs pulses) was --25-fold less than the greatest level observed at 1 MHz (-57.9% at 3.0 MPa, 200/xs pulses). Similarly, with 3.46-MHz ultrasound, hemolysis in- creased generally as P- increased. As at 2.24 MHz, there was no sharp inflection of hemolysis at any P used here (Fig. 4). At a frequency of 1.02 MHz, comparable P- and a constant duty factor of 0.01, hemolysis was gen- erally greater with 200/xs pulses than with 20/xs pulses (Fig. 2). The same trend is evident from the data obtained using 2.24-MHz (Fig. 3) and 3.46-MHz (Fig. 4) ultra- 1.4" 3.46 MHz g._= ,.2. 't '""""t 1.0 .... Hemolysis declined sharply as the ultrasound frequency increased from 1.02 to 2.24 MHz when either 20 ~s (Fig. 5A) or 200/xs (Fig. 5B) pulses were applied. With 20 ~s pulses, hemolysis showed a further decline with increase of frequency to 3.46 MHz only at P- --> 2.0 MPa (Fig. 5A inset). In contrast, when 200 /xs pulses were used, hemolysis declined monotonically with in- creasing frequency over the 1.02-3.46-MHz frequency range (Fig. 5B). The main ANOVA results (Table 2) show signifi- cant differences among the levels of all 3 main effects. In addition, there are significant interactions between fre- quency and pulse duration, and between frequency and peak negative acoustic pressure. The interpretation of the second is that the differences among the 3 ultrasound frequencies change as the applied peak negative acoustic pressure changes. To address the question of a threshold with respect to peak negative acoustic pressure, each of the 6 combi- nations of ultrasound frequency and pulse duration were analyzed separately using 2-way ANOVAs. Each of these treated peak negative pressure as a fixed effect and donor as a random effect. In addition to the ANOVA, a multiple comparison procedure, Dunnett's test, was used to compare each of the upper (nonzero) levels of the peak negative pressure with the lowest control level. The overall error rate for each of the 6 separate sets of comparisons was 5%. For the purpose of this discussion, "threshold" is taken to mean the lowest peak negative pressure at which significant levels of hemolysis are produced, recognizing that the actual threshold must necessarily be somewhat lower than the specified value. The results of these comparison are summarized in Table 3. In Table 3, peak negative pressures are presented using rounded values. The most conservative interpreta- tion of the data obtained using 1.02-MHz ultrasound is that the threshold acoustic pressure is less than 1.5 MPa; however, there is some inconsistency in these data, and the true threshold may be -0.5 MPa under these condi- tions. At 1.02 MHz using 200 /xs pulses, the pressure threshold was -0.5 MPa. With 2.24-MHz ultrasound, the threshold acoustic pressure was --0.5 MPa with either 20/xs or 200/zs pulse durations. With 3.46-MHz ultrasound, the threshold pressure for significant hemo- lysis as determined by this analysis was -2.5 MPa for 20 /zs pulses, but -0.5 MPa for 200/zs pulses. The latter data, thus, suggest that the threshold acoustic pressure for hemolysis increases with increasing ultrasound fre- quency, as expected from theory, and that the threshold pressure may diminish as pulse duration increases. Table 3. Results of two-way ANOVAs in which the peak negative pressure threshold for hemolysis was sought. The thresholds predicted by these models vary according to the assumptions used (e.g., about the size distribution of potential nuclei in the host fluid and whether the bubbles are "free" or "encapsu- lated"). However, each predicts that the threshold pres- sure for cavitation is between 0.1-0.5 MPa at a 1-MHz carrier frequency and, in approximate terms, that the threshold pressure will increase to -1 MPa at a fre- quency of 5 MHz. Given the acoustic pressures used here (P- --< 3 MPa), it is reasonable to conclude that inertial cavitation is the primary mechanism by which hemolysis was produced. Flynn and Church (1988) conducted a theoretical analysis indicating that inertial cavitation activity in- creases upon increasing the pulse duration from 1-5 acoustic cycles. The present data are in qualitative agree- ment with this result, and indicate that hemolysis pro- duced by pulsed ultrasound in human erythrocyte sus- pensions having red cell concentrations similar to those of whole blood and containing a gas-carrier contrast agent varies weakly with pulse duration at constant duty factor (Figs. 2-4) At each ultrasound frequency used, increasing the pulse duration by 10-fold increased hemo- lysis by, at most, -2-fold. This result is qualitatively similar to that reported by Carstensen et al. (1992) in their study of the thresholds for murine lung hemorrhage. As the pulse length increased from 1 to 100 ~s, the thresholds for lung hemorrhage decreased by a factor of -2-4. The present results are also qualitatively similar to previous results obtained in this laboratory for the hemolysis of 40% hematocrit human erythrocytes sus- pensions supplemented with Albunexmicrospheres by 1-MHz ultrasound pulses (Brayman et al. 1996). In that study, no hemolytic threshold was determined, but the levels of hemolysis produced by pulses with durations ranging over -3 orders of magnitude (5-1,000 ~s; duty factor = 0.01) differed by less than 2 orders of magni- tude at the pulse duration extremes. Similarly, duty fac- tor does not appear to be a strong determinant of the extent of bioeffect severity (Brayman, unpublished data; Child et al. 1990). Hemolysis produced by pulsed ultrasound was fre- quency-dependent (Fig. 5A and B). Because each of the transducers used here produced ultrasound beams of somewhat different diameters at the focal plane (Table 1), it is possible that a component of the apparent fre- quency response is related to differences in the volume of the sample insonated at the 3 different frequencies. However, 1.02-MHz pulses having a peak negative acoustic pressure of 3 MPa and a beam diameter of 3.2 mm (Table 1) produced --60% hemolysis (Fig. 2), and 2.24-MHz pulses of comparable acoustic pressure pro- duced only -1.5-2.5% hemolysis (Fig. 3), despite the larger beam diameter (3.7 ram; Table 1) produced by the 2.24-MHz transducer. Thus, ultrasound frequency rather than beam diameter differences appears to dominate the results obtained. The data in Fig. 5A and B suggest that the fre- quency dependence of hemolysis varies with the applied acoustic pressure. At relatively high P-, the frequency dependence appears to be much sharper than that asso- ciated with the inception of cavitation activity (Flynn 1982; Flynn and Church 1988; Apfel and Holland 1990; Roy et al. 1990), lung hemorrhage (Child et al. 1990) or sonochemical hydrogen peroxide formation (Miller and Thomas 1993). The frequency dependence of hemolytic yield at comparable P- was explored by assuming that the extent of cavitation-induced hemolysis at near threshold or su- prathreshold acoustic pressures is proportional to P- divided by the ultrasound frequency raised to some power (i.e., proportional to a quantity similar to the MI) (which is meant to predict thresholds for cavitation). An empirical curve-fitting method was used. The extent of hemolysis observed experimentally at a frequency of 1.02 MHz was used to predict the response at other frequencies, given the negative pressure amplitudes and frequencies used in those experiments. The exponents used in these calculations ranged from 0.5 to 4. The results of this analysis for the data obtained using 200/xs ultrasound pulses are illustrated in Fig. 6. Although at P- of 0.5-1.5 MPa, the fit, as assessed visually between the data and any of the curves is not very good, these data appear to agree qualitatively with the curve defined by the relationship RHf = (p-/fo.5) RHf = 1.o2, where RHf is the relative hemolytic yield at frequency f, P- is as defined previously, and RHf = 1.o2 is the relative hemo- lytic yield at the reference frequency of 1.02 MHz (by definition equal to unity). However, at acoustic pressures of 2.0, 2.5 and 3.0 MPa, the data appear to be reasonably well described by the curves defined by RHf = (P-/f) RHf = 1.o2, RHf = (p-/f3) RHf = 1.o2, and RHf = (p-/f4) x RHf = 1.o2, respectively. Although the data are too few to allow firm quantitative conclusions to be drawn regarding the frequency-dependence of ultra- sound-induced hemolytic extent, this analysis suggests that it is strongly pressure-dependent. Figure 7A and B plots the hemolysis data obtained at the 3 different ultrasound frequencies and various acoustic pressures as a function of the MI for data obtained with 20-/xs pulses (Fig. 7A) or 200 /xs pulses (Fig. 7B). The scale of these figures, however, precludes an appreciation for the fact that, at MI --< 2, hemolysis increases generally with increasing MI. This is illustrated in Fig. 8A and B for data obtained using 20-/xs and 200-/xs pulses, respectively. The data were pooled across ultrasound frequency and were sub- jected to simple regression analysis in an attempt to define the threshold MI. The regression was restricted to experimental observations associated with an MI --< 2.2. "Threshold" was defined as the point at which the regression line for the insonated samples crosses the upper 95% confidence limit of the data obtained from samples in the "double sham" treatment. Figure 8A (20-/xs pulses) shows that over the range of MI --0.3- 2.2, there was a statistically significant correlation be- tween hemolysis and MI. The apparent threshold, as defined above, was MI = 0.025. Figure 8B (200-/xs pulses) shows the same trends, although the slope is somewhat greater than for the 20-/xs pulse data. The apparent threshold for hemolysis as determined from these data was MI = 0.323. The results of this study are encouraging from the perspective of whether or not substantial levels of hemo- lysis are likely to be produced in vivo by diagnostic ultrasound, even in the presence of abundant potential cavitation nuclei (i.e., microbubble contrast agents). As described in the text, the points at which the regression lines cross the upper 95% confidence limit for the "double sham" treatment data are taken to represent the approximate threshold for he- molysis with respect to the mechanical index. highest P- used here are similar to the upper limits of those that can be produced by diagnostic machines (Pat- ton et al. 1994), and the approximate MI values associ- ated with these in vitro exposures at 2.24 and 3.46 MHz were similar to (although somewhat greater than) the maximum value of 1.9 allowed by current regulations (see Meltzer 1996). However, the 200-/xs pulse duration used here is considerably longer than those used in most imaging applications. Despite these extreme insonation conditions, the levels of hemolysis produced at 2.24 and 3.46 MHz were low (<5%). Would levels of hemolysis similar to this have any biological significance were they to occur in vivo? This question is of direct relevance to the issue of ultrasound safety; we attempt to address this issue below. The normal life span of human erythrocytes is 100 days. As the cells age, the cell membrane becomes less elastic and more susceptible to rupture by shear forces experienced during circulation (Firkin et al. 1989). About 80-90% of normal erythrocyte destruction occurs extravascularly (e.g., in the spleen); the remaining 10- 20% occurs intravascularly (Wintrobe et al. 1981). Thus, low-level intravascular hemolysis is a normal occur- rence. At 5 109 cells/mL 6000 mL systemic volume, there are 3 1013 erythrocytes in the body. Given a 100-day lifetime, then 3 10]1 cells are destroyed each day, of which 10-20% are destroyed intravascularly, which is the equivalent of 6-12 mL of blood. The Hgbn content of whole blood is on the order of 150 g/L (Handin et al. 1995), so this level of hemolysis would release 900-1800 mg of free Hgbn into the plasma. The body has several mechanisms by which to clear free Hgbn from the blood, where free Hgbn concentrations are normally very low (i.e., < 1 mg/dL; Wintrobe et al. 1981). Hemoglobin released directly into the plasma dis- sociates (Hoffman et al. 1995). The fate of the free Hgbn depends, in part, on its concentration in the plasma. At normal concentrations, roughly half of the free Hgbn is cleared by the kidneys and the other half is bound to haptoglobin, a plasma protein produced by the liver (Beck 1991). Haptoglobin comprises - 1% of the total plasma protein, and has a Hgbn binding capacity of 100-140 mg Hgbn/dL plasma (Firkin et al. 1989); in subsequent calculations, we will assume a value of 120 mg Hgbn/dL plasma. The haptoglobin/Hgbn complex is cleared from the plasma at a rate of -15 mg Hgbn/dL plasma/h (Harris 1963) and is degraded in the liver parenchyma (Williams et al. 1990). Free Hgbn is also reabsorbed by the kidneys at a rate of -1.4 mg Hgbn/ min (Williams et al. 1990). This rate is so low that, for moderately extensive hemolytic events, one can expect that the haptoglobin binding system will saturate and the renal absorption system will have little immediate effect on the concentration of free Hgbn (i.e., Hgbn remaining unbound to haptoglobin). When the free Hgbn concen- tration exceeds --150 mg/dL plasma, hemoglobinuria may result (Massry and Glassock 1989). For rapid he- molytic events, the threshold for hemoglobinuria should be approached when the concentration of total Hgbn released by hemolysis approaches 120 mg/dL plasma + 150 mg/dL plasma = 270 mg/dL plasma. The volume Hemolysis of Erythrocytes by Ultrasound A. A. BRAYMAN et al. 1247 fraction of erythrocytes in whole blood is typically on the order of 40%. Therefore, a released Hgbn concentration of 270 mg/dL plasma corresponds to 162 mg released/dL whole blood. Assuming a systemic blood volume of 60 dL, then --10 g of Hgbn must be released into the total blood pool to produce hemoglobinuria (this estimate agrees, to within a factor of 2, with experimental obser- vations discussed below). In turn, this would correspond to a systemic level of hemolysis of --1.1%. MW Miller et al. (1995) modeled the passage of a 5-mL bolus of Albunexmicrobubbles through the heart and estimated that, as the bolus passes through the heart, the average Albunexconcentration in the blood is -36 /zL/mL, as was used in this in vitro study. This is equivalent to the microbubble bolus being distributed more or less uniformly in -150 mL of blood. However, while this manuscript was in preparation, a cardiologist (RS Meltzer) having long experience with echocardio- graphic contrast challenged our assumptions regarding the washin/washout kinetics of contrast agents, and in- vited us to review videotaped transesophogeal echocar- diograms obtained following intravenous injection of a 5-10 mL bolus of agitated saline contrast agent. Video- tapes from 4 patients were reviewed. From the time contrast first appeared in the heart to nearly complete clearance required --15 beats for the patient with the most rapid clearance to about 30 beats in the patient with the least rapid clearance. If we assume, for the sake of a "worst-case" scenario, that 30 heart beats are required for contrast washin/washout then, at 50 mL/beat, about 1.5 L of blood/contrast agent--or about 25% of the systemic blood volume--could be insonated during pas- sage through the heart. Thus, our earlier assumptions (MW Miller et al. 1995) appear to have been wrong. One consequence of this is that the simulated in vivo Albu- nexconcentration used in this study is too high; how- ever, this should not materially affect the experimental results, because MW Miller et al. (1995) demonstrated that Albunexenhanced ultrasonic hemolysis is essen- tially independent of Albunexconcentration over a wide range. The following model incorporates the revised, "worst-case" estimate of the volume of blood likely to be insonated during the washin/washout of a contrast bolus (i.e., --1.5 L). As developed above, the conditions used here to insonate blood samples at 2.24- and 3.46- MHz ultrasound frequencies were similar to the "worst- case" exposure conditions likely to be encountered in imaging-related exposures, yet produced only --3% he- molysis in the in vitro samples. Assume for the sake of a "worst-case" scenario, that similar levels of hemolysis might be produced in vivo (i.e., that the blood in the heart is insonated during passage of the contrast agent and results in lysis of 3% of the cells present). This level would be sufficient to saturate the haptoglobin binding system (120 mg Hgbn/dLpl ...... 0.6 dEN .... a/dLblood X 60 dLblood = 4.3 g Hgbn required). At 3% hemolysis in 25% of the systemic blood volume, the equivalent ho- mogeneous level of hemolysis (EHH), after mixing has been achieved, will be about 0.75%. This estimate agrees very well with the results of a recent in vivo study (Dalecki et al. 1997). In that study, the hearts of mice that had been injected with Albunexto an estimated sys- temic concentration of -5%, were exposed to intense pulsed ultrasound of 1.15 or 2.35 MHz frequencies (10-/~s pulses, 0.001 duty factor, 5 min). At 1.15 MHz, pulses having P- similar to those used here (3.6 MPa) produced a systemic level of hemolysis of --4%. At 2.35 MHz, pulses of somewhat higher P- (4.4 MPa) produced a systemic level of hemolysis of -0.5%. Remarkably, these data suggest that, at high P-, the frequency depen- dence of ultrasonic hemolysis in vivo scales in approxi- mate proportion to a P/f relationship, as noted for the data obtained here at similar P-. Were a systemic level of hemolysis of 0.75% to be produced in a patient, as modeled above, this would release --6.7 g of Hgbn into the circulation (0.75% EHH 150 g Hgbn/L blood 6 L blood = 6.75 g Hgbn). As developed, the haptoglobin system would be saturated by 4.3 g Hgbn, leaving 2.4 g of unbound Hgbn in the circulation. In 6 L of blood (3.6 L plasma), this would result in a plasma concentration of -65 mg/dL plasma. Are free Hgbn levels of this order likely to produce biological effects? Hemoglobin is, of course, not the only material liberated into the circulation upon intravascular hemoly- sis. For example, it is uncertain what effect a "bolus" of cellular membrane debris might have on the microcircu- lation. Hemoglobin is, however, a major constituent of the red cells, and there is a substantial literature indicat- ing that free Hgbn released by acute intravascular hemo- lysis may be an important factor in renal failure. Heme proteins are potentially toxic due to their oxidative activity (Nath et al. 1995; Abraham et al. 1995). The kidney is among the organs where this po- tential toxicity may be expressed; acute renal failure associated with hemoglobinemia has been known for decades, often in association with once-common trans- fusion mismatches (see Flamenbaum et al. 1983). He- molytic episodes that exceed the Hgbn-binding capacity of baptoglobin allow Hgbn to be filtered by the kidneys (Bunn et al. 1969), where it is potentially toxic (Jacobson et al. 1991). Glomerular filtration of Hgbn results in reabsorption of the Hgbn until the reabsorption capacity is exceeded. When this occurs, Hgbn may enter the urine; the threshold concentration in plasma for hemo- globinurea is -150 mg/dL plasma (Massry and Glassock 1989). The iron derived from Hgbn reabsorbed from the 1248 Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology Volume 23, Number 8, 1997 blood may be retained for long periods (> 30 days) in the kidneys (Bunn and Jandl 1969). Taking 150 mg Hgbn/dL plasma as the threshold for hemoglobinuria, we estimate that the renal absorption capacity for Hgbn is 150 mg Hgbn/dL plasma 0.6 dL plasma/dL blood X 60 dL blood = -5.5 g of circulating Hgbn above the hapto- globin-binding capacity. This compares to, within an order of magnitude, the results of an experiment con- ducted decades ago (Sears et al. 1966), in which 19 g of Hgbn, derived from filtered, freeze/thawed autologous red cell lysate, were injected into a human volunteer. Urinary Hgbn was monitored for 48 h. Of the 19 g injected, about 4 g are expected to be bound by hapto- globin, leaving --15 g in the circulation. Of this, --2 g were recovered in the urine, suggesting that the renal absorption capacity was --13 g. No ill effects were reported to have occurred in the subject in response to the treatment. Similarly, Savitsky et al. (1978) infused red cell lysate, from which --99% of the membrane lipids had been removed, into human volunteers. The infusion produced hemoglobinuria in all subjects within 30-60 rain. Modest, transient changes in heart rate, urinary output and rate of endogenous creatine clearance were observed, but each effect was reversible. These results further indicate that free Hgbn is well tolerated in normal individuals. Acute nephrotoxicity of Hgbn leading to renal fail- ure appears to be expressed only when other conditions have compromised renal function (e.g., dehydration, ac- idosis, or other toxins) (Jacobson et al. 1991). A variety of mechanisms for Hgbn-associated renal failure, under the conditions cited above, has been proposed. These include: 1. Tubular necrosis due to direct toxicity of Hgbn breakdown products (e.g., ferrihemate produced under acidic conditions, Massry and Glassock 1989; Flamenbaum et al. 1983); 2. renal ischemia due to the vasoconstrictive activity of heme proteins (Massry and Glassock, 1989; Dubrow and Flamenbaum 1988); 3. heme protein-mediated membrane lipid peroxidation (Nath et al. 1995); and 4. blockage of the nephrons due to precipitation/polymerization reactions between the globin of Hgbn and acidic mucoproteins (Massry and Glassock 1989). However, as developed above, low- level intravascular hemolysis in patients without other renal compromise seems unlikely to have large effects on renal function. Sears et al. (1966) reported on 12 patients with hemolytic disorders, including several patients in whom mechanical hemolysis arose via suboptimal per- formance of a replacement aortic valve. None of the patients had detectable levels of haptoglobin in the blood plasma. The free plasma Hgbn concentrations ranged from 0-29 mg/dL plasma, and yet there was no evidence of gross renal function abnormality in any of the patients. A plasma Hgbn concentration of 30 mg/dL above the haptoglobin-binding capacity is about half of the free Hgbn concentration our "worst-case" model predicts may be present in the plasma following ultrasonic he- molysis and saturation of the haptoglobin system. The text above indicates that acute renal failure is quite unlikely unless there are other problems with kid- ney function. However, the results of Nath et al. (1995), obtained using in vivo and in vitro models, indicate that Hgbn has demonstrable renal toxicity at concentrations on the order of a few hundred mg/dL. Using an in vivo rat kidney model, they demonstrated that, within 30 rain of methemoglobin administration (30 rag/100 g body weight), the glomerular filtration rate and renal blood flow rates were reduced by a factor of -2; these events were associated with an approximately 2-fold increase in the calculated renal vascular resistance. In similar exper- iments, Jaenike (1966) injected rats with 40 mg hemo- globin/100 g body weight; plasma hemoglobin concen- trations ranged from 400-600 mg/dL within 20-30 rain following the injection. (In humans, to achieve this con- centration above the haptoglobin-binding capacity would require about 3.4% hemolysis in the systemic blood pool.) Thus, the heme protein impaired renal function and was attended by renal ischemia, a condition long known to sensitize the kidney to Hgbn toxicity. This may arise, in part, by ischemic enhancement of consumption of cellular glutathione stores, thus producing a prooxi- dant cytoplasmic redox state (McCoy et al. 1988). The in vitro studies of Nath et al. (1995), using renal epithelial cells, demonstrated that 20 /xM methemoglobin (i.e. --130 mg/dL) was without demonstrable cytotoxic effect on normal cells as assessed by a 51Cr release assay. However, when the cells were pretreated with a reagent that depleted cellular glutathione, thus mimicking the effects of renal ischemia, the specific toxicity of the heme protein was increased nearly 2-fold relative to controls. The authors conclude that "a prooxidant state, induced by the reduction of glutathione levels, renders heme proteins toxic to the kidney." It is noteworthy that Hgbn has been demonstrated to have toxic effects on other cell types in vitro. Abraham et al. (1995) have shown that the viability of rabbit coronary microvessel endothelial cells in vitro decreases monotonically as the concentration of free Hgbn in the culture medium in- creases over the range of 1-400 /xM. About 15% mor- tality was observed at a Hgbn concentration of 100/xM (-650 mg/dL); this mortality was reduced significantly by enforced overexpression of heme oxygenase, an en- zyme of central importance in heme catabolism. This finding is consistent with a mechanism of cellular dam- age involving heme-induced oxidative damage. Balla et al. (1993) studied the relationship between oxidant-mediated damage to aortic endothelial cells in vitro and Hgbn. Reduced ferrohemoglobin was without Hemolysis of Erythrocytes by Ultrasound A. A. BRAYMAN et al. 1249 significant cytotoxic effect on cultured endothelial cells. However, the oxidized (ferri- or met-hemoglobin) form significantly enhanced cell injury by H202. Using the 51Cr release assay of cytotoxicity, they exposed the cells to heme or heine proteins for 1 h prior to challenge of the cells with 100 /xM H202. Preincubation with 10 /xM methemoglobin (--60 mg/dL) produced a 3-fold increase in specific cytotoxicity due to H20~. The enhancement of HzO2-induced toxicity by methemoglobin was partially overcome by free heme scavengers or by haptoglobin; the latter strengthens the association between heme and globin. Thus, the evidence indicated that free heme was being released by the methemoglobin. There is also evidence that oxidants can liberate free, ferrous iron from Hgbn (Siebert et al. 1991), which can, in turn, catalyze the formation of hydroxyl radicals ('OH) from H202. Siebert et al. (1991) studied Hgbn enhancement of oxi- dant-induced injury to perfused, ventilated excised rat lungs. An organic peroxide (tert-butyl hydroperoxide) was used as the oxidant challenge. This agent alone caused lung tissue injury, and the level of injury was enhanced significantly by the presence of 50 ~M free Hgbn in the perfusate; this was associated with a 3-4- fold increase in free iron in the perfusate relative to when Hgbn was absent. The authors note that several other studies have indicated that intact erythrocytes can protect cells in vitro and in vivo from H202-induced damage, and attribute this to the facts that Hgbn is compartmen- talized within the red cells (i.e., not free in solution) and that the red cells contain abundant antioxidant enzymes. These observations naturally lead one to question what might happen in the case of ultrasonic hemolysis accom- panied by sonochemical production of free radicals and/or H202 (see DL Miller et al. 1995). One might speculate that, on the one hand, perhaps the released Hgbn would react with the sonochemical species pro- duced in the near vicinity of the lysed cells because both events are expected to arise from cavitation events. Were this to occur, catalytic iron might be released from the free Hgbn, and this iron could, in turn, catalyze the formation of injurious free radicals. On the other hand, release of the cytoplasmic antioxidant enzymes might effectively consume many of the sonochemicals. Of course, there are too many imponderables to allow even a guess as to the likely answer to this question, but it remains an interesting one. The foregoing analysis, although relying upon a superficial overview of an extensive literature, suggests that even in the "worst case" scenario, intravascular hemolysis potentially produced by ultrasonic interroga- tion of the heart with contrast agent present would not be grossly hazardous to a patient. In this scenario, it appears possible that the haptoglobin system might be exhausted, and that a modest level of free Hgbn would remain in the circulation. This level would be almost certainly too low to result in either observable hemoglobinuria or gross impairment of renal function. However, clinical studies of this issue have not been conducted, and it is possible that the physiological effects of ultrasound-induced in- travascular hemolysis may be greater in patients with pre-existing health problems. Moreover, the possibility of subtle toxicity effects can not be ruled out. Acknowledgements--This study was supported by USPHS Grant Num- ber CA39230-23. item-info.ce:copyright: World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology

head.ce:title: Hemolysis of 40% hematocrit, Albunex®-supplemented human erythrocytes by pulsed ultrasound: Frequency, acoustic pressure and pulse length dependence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology The University of Rochester, Rochester, NY 14642 USA
Division of Biostatistics The University of Rochester, Rochester, NY 14642 USA
Rochester Center for Biomedical Ultrasound, The University of Rochester, Rochester, NY 14642 USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Address for correspondence: Andrew A. Brayman, Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, School of Medicine and Dentistry, The University of Rochester, Rochester, NY 14642 USA.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The dependence of hemolysis produced by pulsed ultrasound on ultrasound frequency, acoustic pressure and pulse length was explored. Human erythrocytes (40% hematocrit; in Albunex®-supplemented autologous plasma) were exposed (60 s) to 20 or 200 μs pulses of ultrasound at frequencies of 1.02, 2.24 or 3.46 MHz and at peak negative pressures [P−] ranging from 0.0 to ∼3.0 MPa in 0.5 MPa increments. The duty factor was 0.01. At each frequency, hemolysis increased with increasing acoustic pressure and depended weakly on pulse duration. At relatively high acoustic pressures, hemolysis depended strongly on ultrasound frequency; at lower pressures, the frequency dependence was weaker. The potential clinical significance of ultrasonic hemolysis is discussed.
